mappings.rationaleAI: J.J. Thomson's 1897 identification of the electron as a component of all matter (A.3.c), its role in atomic structure and chemical bonding (A.1), and quantum mechanical electron behaviour including wave-particle duality (A.4.c).
Rutherford's discovery of the proton and its role defining atomic number addresses the nucleus-electron structure (A.1), while its connection to the Rutherford model is central to A.4.a.
Rutherford's gold foil experiment established the nuclear model of the atom (A.4.a), and his identification of alpha and beta radiation relates to radioactive transformation (A.3.e).

The Exclusion Principle explains electron shell configurations and periodic table structure via quantum states, directly addressing wave-mechanical electronic structure (A.4.c) and the origin of atomic spectra in quantized transitions (C.2).
The uncertainty principle is foundational to the wave-mechanical theory of electronic structure (A.4.c) and shapes understanding of quantized electronic transitions producing atomic spectra (C.2).

Dirac's prediction of the positron, its experimental discovery, matter-antimatter symmetry, annihilation reactions, CP violation, and the baryon asymmetry problem comprehensively address all aspects of antimatter (E.1-E.4).
Dirac's prediction of antimatter through the Dirac equation and the positron's confirmation address antimatter properties (E.1) and charge conjugation invariance (E.3).
X-ray diffraction crystallography by the Braggs directly addresses general X-ray phenomena (D.1), X-ray theory and atomic structure (D.2), and diffraction of X rays by crystals (D.5).
Dalton's atomic theory with characteristic atomic weights bridges philosophical atomism (A.2) and chemical evidence for atoms (A.3.a), and his atomic weight tables directly address B.

The history from Greek four-element concepts through Mendeleev's periodic table covers early atomistic speculation (A.2), chemical developments (A.3.a), and the significance of atomic weights in chemistry (B.2).

Einstein's Brownian motion paper provided evidence for atoms (A.3.f), his photoelectric effect work connects to X-ray and photon phenomena (D.2), and his work relates to fundamental physical constants (F).
Cosmic rays' role in discovering subatomic particles (positron, muon, pion) through high-energy atmospheric collisions addresses the production of antiparticles in high-energy collisions (E.2).

The photoelectric effect, Planck's quantum hypothesis, and wave-particle duality address the significance of atomic spectra (C.1), quantized electronic transitions (C.2), and the relationship between photon absorption and atomic structure (D.2.a).
The speed of light as a fundamental physical constant, its measurement history, and its role in E=mc2 directly address the fundamental physical constants (F) and their interrelationships (F.2).
